text: A ten millimeter port is placed in the right or left lower quadrant to facilitate laparoscopic suturing, allowing easy admission of a CT-one needle into the abdomen. The valve system of the port is crucial for performing extracorporeal knot tying.

The surgeon demonstrates techniques for introducing a suture needle into the abdomen. In the first method, the suture is grasped 2-3 cm from the needle, and the needle driver is used to insert the needle. In the second method, the needle driver in the non-dominant hand grasps the heel of the needle, positioning the point alongside the driver for insertion. The needle is then placed into the abdomen, where it can be grasped with another needle driver.

The surgeon passes the needle into the abdomen preloaded on a driver, grasping it approximately halfway between the heel and the point. Gentle traction on the suture allows for small adjustments in needle positioning.

The surgeon uses the dominant hand to grasp the needle at its midpoint and rotates it using the suture, securing the tissue efficiently. The tissue is elevated and stabilized with either the non-dominant hand or an assistant as the suture is placed. Proper needle positioning is crucial due to the limitations of standard laparoscopic instruments compared to robotic ones.

The surgeon secures the suture using extracorporeal knot tying with a 36-inch suture and a knot pusher. The closed-ended knot pusher, with a hole for threading the suture, is used to slide the knot into the port and pelvis, approximating the tissue.

The surgeon ensures the knot lies flat by pulling the knot pusher back without removing it from the suture. The knot pusher is laid aside while maintaining tension on the suture with one hand holding the clamp. Successive knots are thrown and pushed down, alternating the direction of each throw to create square knots.
The surgeon uses a knot pusher to untwist a suture that has become twisted, rotating the knot pusher around the knot until the twist is removed, ensuring the suture lays flat.

The surgeon navigates the suture around a pelvic structure, carefully avoiding adjacent tissue. The knot is initially pushed away from the structure, then gently slid down by maintaining tension on the suture and angling the knot pusher.

The surgeon uses the open knot pusher technique, placing the knot pusher on the suture between each throw. The clamp remains on one end of the suture while the surgeon holds both ends in one hand, maintaining gentle tension. The open knot pusher is applied above the knot, which is then pushed down into the pelvis and laid flat.

The surgeon performs successive alternating throws of the knot using an open knot pusher, ensuring the knot is complete. Occasionally, the knot pusher may slip off the suture before the knot is fully secured.

The surgeon passes the suture through the tissue and prepares by grasping the needle tip with the non-dominant hand, aligning the needle parallel to the needle driver. The suture is wrapped twice around the needle driver to form a surgeon's knot. The driver then grasps the free end of the suture, and the knot is tightened, ensuring it lies flat on the tissue.

The surgeons alternate the direction of each throw, laying the knots flat until the knot is complete. In the two-person technique, one surgeon presents the needle with the suture parallel to the other's needle driver for the first throw. The free end is grasped and the knot is tightened, ensuring adequate length for future knots. The surgeons then switch roles and repeat the procedure.
